coredata.dc:description: The porosities and tortuosities are commonly utilised to characterise the microstructure of a Li-ion battery's separator and are adopted as key input parameters in advanced battery models. Herein, a general classification of the tortuosity for a porous medium is introduced based on its dual significance, i.e. the geometrical and physical tortuosities. Then, three different methods for the determination of the separator's electrical tortuosity are introduced and compared, which include the porosity-tortuosity relationship using analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations, the experimental method using Electrochemical Impedance Spectrum testing and the numerical method using realistic 3D microstructure of the separator obtained from nanoscale X-ray computed tomography. In addition, the connection between the geometrical tortuosity and the electrical tortuosity of a separator is established by introducing the electrical phenomenological factor, which can facilitate the understanding of the relationship between the microstructure characteristics and transport properties of the separators. Furthermore, to quantitatively compare the values of the tortuosities determined by different methods, the corresponding effective transport coefficients are compared, which was usually used as a correction for the effective diffusivity and conductivity of electrolytes in porous media.

head.ce:title: Determination of the tortuosity of a Li-ion battery separator

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The porosities and tortuosities are commonly utilised to characterise the microstructure of a Li-ion battery's separator and are adopted as key input parameters in advanced battery models. Herein, a general classification of the tortuosity for a porous medium is introduced based on its dual significance, i.e. the geometrical and physical tortuosities. Then, three different methods for the determination of the separator's electrical tortuosity are introduced and compared, which include the porosity-tortuosity relationship using analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations, the experimental method using Electrochemical Impedance Spectrum testing and the numerical method using realistic 3D microstructure of the separator obtained from nanoscale X-ray computed tomography. In addition, the connection between the geometrical tortuosity and the electrical tortuosity of a separator is established by introducing the electrical phenomenological factor, which can facilitate the understanding of the relationship between the microstructure characteristics and transport properties of the separators. Furthermore, to quantitatively compare the values of the tortuosities determined by different methods, the corresponding effective transport coefficients are compared, which was usually used as a correction for the effective diffusivity and conductivity of electrolytes in porous media.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Li-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely adopted as energy storage devices in various applications due to their high energy density and good electrochemical performance. A basic LIB cell consists of two porous electrodes coated on metallic current collectors and a porous polymeric separator [1]. The porous separator filled with electrolyte allows the Li-ion to move between the electrodes while preventing an internal short circuit from direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes. To accurately evaluate the safety, performance and degradation of LIB at multiple scales, it is necessary to ensure that the ion transport through the battery's separator can be reliably described in a battery model.

Homogenised multi-physics battery models have been widely adopted to predict battery performance with high efficiency [2–7], among which the model proposed by Newman [3] has been widely used and has been implemented in COMOSL Multiphysics software where the porous electrodes and separator are homogenised using the macroscopic description of porous media. The homogenised battery model considers the effect of the separator's microstructure on the macroscopic performance of LIB using two important characterisation parameters of the microstructure, i.e. porosity ( Φ ) and tortuosity ( τ ), to estimate the effective diffusivity (
D eff
) and effective conductivity (
σ eff
) of the separator immersed in the electrolyte [7], i.e.
(1)
D eff
=
Φ
τ 2
∙
D 0
(2)
σ eff
=
Φ
τ 2
∙
σ 0
where
D 0
and
σ 0
are respectively the bulk electrolyte diffusivity and bulk electrolyte conductivity.

In Eqs. (1) and (2), porosity has been well-defined and can be measured accurately. However, there are large uncertainties in the definition and determination of tortuosity, which plays an important role in mass transfer, charge transfer and ohmic loss in a LIB [1,8]. Many analytical and empirical equations have been developed to determine the tortuosity using a porosity-tortuosity relationship summarised in [9]. Among these, the analytical Maxwell [10] and empirical Bruggeman equations [11,12] are most widely used in the field of electrochemistry, which are given by
(3)
τ Maxwell 2
=
3 − Φ
2
and
(4)
τ Brugg 2
=
Φ
1 − α
respectively, where α is the Bruggeman exponent assumed to be 1.5 in its standard form. Hoogschagen [13] applied the above two equations to corroborate the results of diffusion experiments involving gas permeation through the glass spheres. The findings revealed that the tortuosity values obtained experimentally were laid between those predicted by the Maxwell and Bruggeman equations but leaned more towards the latter's estimations [14]. This has led to the prevalent adoption of the Bruggemen equation in the subsequent studies on the effective transport characteristics of porous media in LIB, such as separators and electrodes [3,7,15–17]. However, it is crucial to note that the underpinnings of both Maxwell and Bruggeman equations rest on the assumption of presence of non-conductive spherical elements within the porous media, which differs greatly from the more complex pore geometries found in separators. Therefore, Maxwell and Bruggeman equations should be further evaluated for their applicability to separators.

Two experimental approaches have been developed to evaluate the tortuosity of separators. The first approach employs the impedance-based technique to measure separator's conductivity or resistivity socked in an electrolyte [18–26], while the second uses the polarisation-interrupt method to measure the diffusivity of a separator immersed in an electrolyte [21]. Emerging evidence suggests that the microstructural design of separators plays a critical role in influencing the capacity performance of LIBs [18,20,24,27]. Notably, Parikh et al. [24] have revealed that LIBs with Celgard 2500 separators demonstrated a 57 % and 47 % higher capacity performance at 2 and 3C-rates, respectively, compared to those using Celgard 2325 separators. The reported porosities of Celgard 2500 and Celgard 2325 are 55 % and 39 %, respectively, while their tortuosities are 1.70 and 1.98, respectively. Furthermore, researchers conducted electrochemical tests on LIBs with four different types of separators to assess their capacity performance [27]. The results revealed that the assembled LIBs using separators with higher porosity tended to exhibit relatively lower battery capacities, which, however, is different from the result in our existing study [28]. The researchers concluded that the low capacity performance could be attributed to two factors, i.e. the initial loss of lithium and the electrolyte's decomposition, leading to the formation of interphase layers. However, evaluating a separator's transport properties based solely on its porosity and thickness in [27] is insufficient. The separator's complex microstructure and its influence on transport require the consideration of the separator's tortuosity. Therefore, to holistically evaluate the transport properties of separators and their influences on LIB's performance, it is imperative to consider not only porosity and thickness but also the tortuosity of the separator. Incorporating the tortuosity parameter enables a more accurate assessment of the separator's intricate microstructure and its influence on ion transport, providing a more profound understanding of LIB's performance.

More interestingly, it has been observed that the tortuosity values for identical separators, such as Celgard 2500, reported from different studies show significant variations, with different values of 3.2 [18], 2.16 [19], 2.5 [20], 1.58 [23] and 1.7 [24]. This implies a notable inconsistency in the determination of separator's tortuosity as a crucial parameter for the homogenised battery model. The reliability of these measurements is further questioned due to the missing details in experimental setups, such as cell design, separator thickness, electrode dimensions, electrolyte choice and temperature at test. While different measurement setups, including coin-cell [19,20,24,25], pouch-cell [21–23,26] and self-designed copper block [22,23] formats, have been adopted, there is a gap in comparative analysis across these and other methods for determining the tortuosity of separators.

With the rapid development of imaging technology, researchers started to determine the tortuosity of a separator numerically using the reconstruction of the 3D microstructure of the separator from nanoscale X-ray computed tomography (XCT) [29,30] and the nanoscale XCT combined with either focused ion beam-scanning electron microscope (FIB-SEM) [8,31] or SEM [32]. It is noted that the porosity of the separator Celgard 2500 (53.3 %) obtained from nanoscale XCT is lower than the value of the manufacturer's specification (55 %) [29,30], which is counterintuitive because the porosity measured from XCT should be larger than the manufacturer's porosity obtained from porosity tests since some fine fibrils of the separator cannot be captured by nanoscale XCT due to its intrinsic resolution limitation. Xu et al. [32] tried to extend the above limitation by reconstructing the microstructure of the separator by adding fibrils via the stochastic method based on the XCT dataset and the SEM surface observation. It was found that the transport properties of the separator are sensitive to the number of fibrils added to the microstructure of the separator, which will have a substantial impact on the fast-charging model of LIB [17]. Unfortunately, the correct number of added fibrils cannot be determined in their study due to the lack of reliable reference values of the tortuosity, e.g. the values determined by reliable experimental methods. As a result, it is worthwhile to establish a connection between the microstructural geometry and the macroscopic performance of the porous separator. Furthermore, due to the thin-thickness nature of the separator in the through-thickness direction (TTD) (i.e. through-plane direction), the existing experimental method can only be used to determine the tortuosity of a separator along the TTD. However, the tortuosities along both the TTD and the in-plane direction of a separator can be determined by the numerical method using the separator's 3D microstructure. Therefore, it is necessary to examine the correlation between the tortuosity determined from the experimental method and the tortuosity determined from the microstructure of the separator, which can be used to obtain the tortuosities along in-plane-directions numerically when the experimental measurement is practically impossible.

To fill the above gaps and improve the understanding of the role of tortuosity in the function of a separator, three independent methods for determining the tortuosity of the dry-processed separator Celgard 2500 are introduced and evaluated. Firstly, the definitions of tortuosity in the field of porous materials are discussed considering its dual significance. Then, the analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations based on the porosity-tortuosity relationship were employed to determine the tortuosity of the separator. Subsequently, the tortuosity of the separator was experimentally determined using impedance testing, from which highly consistent impedance results were achieved. Furthermore, the tortuosities along TTD and in-plane directions were determined based on the reconstruction of the 3D microstructure obtained from nanoscale XCT. The effective transport coefficient is introduced in this study to compare those tortuosities determined by different methods quantitatively. Finally, based on the obtained tortuosities from impedance testing and the simulation method using the XCT image along the TTD, the tortuosities of the separator along in-plane directions are analytically predicted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Definition of tortuosity
ce:section.ce:para: Since multiple definitions of tortuosity and various determination methods have been introduced in different literatures, which may confuse the readers, it is necessary to clarify the definitions of tortuosity before describing the methodology used in this study. Generally, tortuosity describes the sinuousness and interconnectedness of the pore space in a porous medium, which is a key factor in controlling the transport process through the porous medium. Although tortuosity has its unique geometrical significance, its determination is usually associated with a particular physical process, i.e. it has a dual significance. It implies that the macroscopic physical behaviour of a porous medium is intrinsically determined by its geometrical microstructure and the occurrence of the physical process in the microstructure. In academic literature, tortuosity is described as either a geometrical characteristic or parameter linked to a specific physical process, e.g., a hydraulic, electrical, or diffusive mechanism [9]. Therefore, tortuosity is both a geometrical and physical characteristic of the porous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometrical tortuosity and determination method
ce:section.ce:para:
Geometrical tortuosity is typically defined by the ratio between the effective length of the actual path and the direct line length
L
in the overall flow direction [9,33]. As shown in Fig. 1 , the flow within a porous medium is influenced by its pore channels, leading to the use of shortest flow path (
L g
) as the effective flow path (
L h
) in determining the geometrical tortuosity
τ g
, which can be expressed as [34].
(5)
τ g
=
L h
L
=
L g
L

Hence, the geometrical tortuosity is frequently viewed as a reflection of the sinuosity and interconnectedness of a porous medium's microstructure. Various image-based analysis methods and associated algorithms have been used to determine the geometrical tortuosity from the reconstructed 2D/3D microstructure of the porous media, such as the direct shortest path searching method (DSPSM) [35], the skeleton shortest-path search method (SSPSM) [36], the fast-marching method (FMM) [37,38] and the pore centroid method (PCM) [39,40]. These methods are typically applied directly to the pixel or voxel image data, offering straightforward implementations and computational efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical tortuosities
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to the geometrical tortuosity characterising the microstructure of the porous medium, the physical tortuosities are related to a specific macroscopic physical transport process or phenomena, such as the transport of mass, charge, energy or other physical quantities. Therefore, depending on the actual physical transport phenomenon in the porous medium, various physics-based tortuosities, which are termed as physical tortuosities in the present study, were introduced for different physical transport mechanisms, e.g. the hydraulic, electrical, diffusional and thermal tortuosities. Herein, since the ionic conducting transport in the electrolyte-filled pore phase of the separator is concerned, while the solid phase of the separator is assumed to be non-conducting, the electrical tortuosity of the pore phase will be the focus of the study. Therefore, in this context, geometrical porosity, defined as the ratio of void space (pores filled with electrolytes) to total space, can be directly correlated with the porosity related to the electric conduction. However, the methodology developed in this study is generally applicable to other physical tortuosities. Detailed and systematic reviews on physical tortuosities can be found in [9,33,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separators and electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: This study adopts the Celgard 2500, a commercial separator made from polypropylene (PP), fabricated via a dry process of uniaxial stretching along the machine direction (MD). A more detailed manufacturing process for Celgard 2500 can be found in [44]. This porous separator has a monolayer structure with a nominal thickness of 25 μm and a porosity of 55 % provided by the manufacturer [29].

The electrolyte solution, comprising 1 M LiPF6 in a 3:7 volume ratio of ethylene carbonate (EC) and ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), was procured from ‘dodochem.net’ for impedance measurement. In the present study, the electrolyte selection was referred to the work of the Celgard 2325 separator [23], in which its tortuosity was determined across five different electrolyte concentrations corresponding to various bulk electrolyte conductivities. It was found that very low bulk electrolyte conductivities (∼0.041 S m−1) are susceptible to impurities, whereas high bulk electrolyte conductivities (1.25 S m−1) can result in signal interferences from cable inductance and contact resistances [23]. Therefore, the optimal range for bulk electrolyte conductivities is identified as 0.3 to 1.0 S m−1. Although this recommendation was derived from experiments on the Celgard 2325 (a trilayer separator consisting of PP/ polyethene(PE)/PP with a nominal porosity of 0.39 and a thickness of 25 μm), it is transferable to the Celgard 2500 used in the present study considering that both Celgard 2325 and Celgard 2500 are made from non-conducting PP or PE materials, and only the pore phase facilitates ionic transport. The bulk electrolyte conductivity used in our experiment is 0.830 S m−1 [45]. Therefore, the adopted electrolyte is appropriate for determining the electrical tortuosity of the separator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the electrical tortuosity using the experimental method
Thickness and porosity measurement
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the tortuosity of a separator, the thickness and porosity of the separator need to be measured experimentally. To accurately measure the thickness of the separator, a digital micrometre with a precision of 1 μm was used according to the standard procedure (TAPPI T441 method). To ensure the accuracy and consistency of the thickness measurement, the top ratchet of the micrometre will stop when the contact force reaches 5–10 N, which is equivalent to 0.16 to 0.32 MPa and far from plateau stress (∼ 20 MPa) of the separator under compression [28]. The measurement was repeated three times, determining the separator's thickness as 25.06 ± 0.05 μm. This measurement was the average of 40 layers of the separators combined.

The porosity was measured at an ambient temperature of 21 °C according to the following procedure, i.e. (i) The separator is punched into a circular disk with a diameter of 20 mm; (ii) The mass of the separator disk (
w 1
) is measured in the air environment using a semi-micro analytical balance (Pioneer PX5 from OHAUS) with an accuracy of 0.00001 g; (iii) The mass of the separator disk immersed in the absolute ethanol (
w 2
) is also measured; (iv) The porosity ( Φ ) of the separator is obtained according to Archimedes' principle, which states that a solid body immersed in a fluid apparently loses weight by an amount equal to that of the fluid it displaces, using the following equation [46].
(6)
Φ = 1 −
θ
w 1
−
w 2
ρ 1
−
ρ 2
/
V s
where θ is the balance correction factor (0.99985) when the air buoyancy of the adjustment weight is considered;
ρ 1
= 0.7886 g cm−3 is the density of absolute ethanol at 21 °C;
ρ 2
= 0.0012 g cm−3 is the air density;
V s
is the macroscopic volume of the single-layered separator disk.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coin cell fabrication and impedance measurements
ce:section.ce:para: As described in Eq. (2), in order to determine the electrical tortuosity of the separator Celgard 2500 along the TTD, it is essential to measure the separator's effective conductivity when it is immersed in the electrolyte. Therefore, Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the use of a specifically configured coin cell with the separator to measure the ionic resistance induced by the separator [24]. In addition, to quantify the resistance aroused by the component of the coin cell, an empty coin cell without a separator (but with identical rest parts) was adopted and illustrated in Fig. 2. (b). The detailed experimental procedures are described below.

The single-layered and stack-layered separators (25 μm to 100 μm) were punched to produce circular disks with 18 mm diameter. It is worthwhile to note that the stack-layered separators were folded together to reach the required layered numbers before being punched. Subsequently, the single-layered or stack-layered separators wetted with an electrolyte solution of 1 M LiPF6 in EC/EMC (3:7 v:v) were placed inside the CR2032 coin cell between the two stainless steel spacers with a nominal thickness of 1 mm. The spring was placed between the stainless-steel spacer and the negative case. The coin-cell cases, stainless steel spacers, and springs were obtained from MTI Corporation. The coin cell was fabricated in an argon-saturated atmosphere within a Pure Lab HE Glovebox, ensuring oxygen and water vapour levels were not higher than 0.1 ppm. During the assembly process, the coin cells were crimped using MSK-110 in the pressure range of 500–700 psi (∼ 3.48 to 4.83 MPa) to ensure the spacers did not move. Since the separator of Celgard 2500 only has small compressive deformation when subjected to this level of pressure, as shown in our previous compression tests on the same separator [30], the induced compressive deformation can be reasonably neglected. Three identical coin cells were assembled for each thickness of the separator to achieve consistency. The impedances of the assembled coin cells and the empty cells were then measured by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) test using an electrochemical workstation CS-310 from CorrTest. EIS tests were performed with a frequency range of 1 kHz to 1 MHz with a 5-mV voltage amplitude. By assuming that the solid polymer phase of the separator is non-conductive, the impedance measurements of the configured coin cells can be described by an equivalent circuit consisting of a capacitance, an ionic resistance of the electrolyte-saturated separator (
R ion
), an electrical resistance of the coin cell's component (
R component
) and an interfacial resistance between electrode and electrolyte (
R interfacial
), which can be added up to coin cell's overall resistance (
R cell
). Therefore, the measured resistance of the coin cell can be expressed as [22,25].
(7)
R cell
=
R component
+
R interfacial
+
R ion

ce:section.ce:para:

In Eq. (12),

It encompasses the resistances of the coin cell components (empty cell) (including the spring, two spacers, and both positive and negative cases) and the interfacial resistance between spacers and electrolytes when the thickness of the electrolytes (

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the tortuosity using the 3D image-based method
3D image-based reconstruction of the separator microstructure
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure of the separator was reconstructed using 3D image data obtained from nanoscale XCT in Zernike Phase Contrast mode. The XCT scanning of the separator sample was performed in the Zeiss Xradia 810 Ultra CT system housed at Henry Moseley X-ray Imaging Facilities (HMXIF, The University of Manchester). A 5.4 keV quasi-monochromatic beam, an effective voxel size of 129 nm and an exposure time of 30 s for each of 509 projections over 180 degrees were used. The raw image dataset was reorientated to ensure that the surface of the separator was perpendicular to the TTD of the separator. Then, the automated segmentation method, i.e. the ISODATA method embedded in the Avizo package, was applied to segment the polymer phase of the separator and distinguish it from the surrounding air phase. The detailed setup of the XCT scanning and segmentation method can be found in [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometrical tortuosity determined using the pore centroid method (PCM)
ce:section.ce:para:
As introduced in Section 2.1, there are various methods to determine the geometrical tortuosity of the porous medium. The PCM module in commercial software Avizo was used in this study to determine the geometrical tortuosity of the porous separator due to its simplicity and straightforwardness. The PCM determines the geometrical tortuosity by averaging the shifts in pore centroid locations across the 2D layers within a 3D microstructure along a specified direction [39,40,48]. Fig. 3 illustrates the tortuosity of a pathway formed by the pore centroids on each plane along the z-axis of a binary 3D image. The PCM module first computes the pore centroid coordinate (
c i
= (
x i
,
y i
,
z i
) ) on each plane of the image (
z i
) and then linking these pore centroids on each plane. Then, the geometrical tortuosity of the 3D microstructure can be determined as the ratio of the total path length (
L g

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical tortuosity determined using the 3D image-based method
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the electrical tortuosity of a porous medium, several techniques for simulating electrical conduction in porous media, including finite difference method (FDM) [49], the random walk method (RWM) [50], the finite element method (FEM) [51–55], the lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) [56] and the finite volume method (FVM) [57], are detailed in [41]. This study employed Avizo's Xlab package, i.e. Formation Factor Experiment Simulation Module, to simulate the electric charge transport through the separator's microstructure. The solver of Avizo is based on the FVM [58], with image voxels as volume elements. The separator's polymer solid phase is considered to be uniform and non-conductive, while its pore phase is filled with electrolyte with conductivity (
σ 0
). As shown in Fig. 4 , a fixed electrical potential difference is defined on the input and output planes of the separator's cubic microstructure (the main direction of the flux is perpendicular to the input and output planes), while the planes of the other four sides of the separator's cubic microstructure are set as an electrical insulator allowing the isolation of the fluxes within the sample, i.e. there is no leakage of the flux from the sides of the sample, which is consistent with the designed tests in Fig. 2. The boundary condition at the pore-solid interface is
(16)
∇ →
V · n = 0
where
∇ →
V is the local gradient of the electrical potential (in V m−1) and n is the unit vector normal to the pore-solid interface directed from the pore phase to the solid phase.

The input and output current fluxes become identical when the equilibrium is reached. By applying the charge conservation and Ohm's law on the entire volume, the effective conductivity (
σ eff
in S m−1 or A⋅(V·m)−1) can be calculated by
(17)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Analytical and empirical determination of the tortuosity
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations, i.e. Eqs. (3) and (4), the obtained tortuosity values are respectively 1.11 and 1.16 by inputting the porosity obtained from the manufacturer, which is 55 % for Celgard 2500. It is important to understand the limitation of the Maxwell and Bruggeman equations, i.e. the microstructure difference is not considered when the porosities of the separators are the same. Practically, separators' tortuosities could be very different due to their different microstructures, even though they have similar porosities. This limitation mainly comes from their original derivations, which were analytically or empirically determined by the experiments designed for spherical beans as the solid phase. However, the microstructure of the separator does not ideally meet the designated scenario used to derive Maxwell and Bruggeman equations. Therefore, it is indispensable to determine the tortuosity of a separator using the experimental or numerical method, which will be further discussed in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental determination of the electrical tortuosity based on the method in Section 2.4
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the porosity given by the supplier, the density method was employed to measure the porosity of the separator in this study. The obtained experimental results are listed in Table 1 . Using Eq. (6), the porosity of the separator is calculated as 52.0 % ± 0.9 %, which is close to the nominal porosity (55 %) provided by the supplier.

The ionic resistance of the electrolyte-saturated separator was determined by EIS testing. It is necessary to ensure that sufficient electrolyte is added to wet the single-layer or stack-layered separators when the separators are sandwiched between the two spacers assembled in the coin cell. To achieve repeatable and consistent results, separators need to be thoroughly wetted, for which the tester needs to wait for the separator to become transparent after adding the electrolyte. The impedance spectra of configurated coin and empty cells are presented using Nyquist plots in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). The overall resistance of the coin cell
R cell
and the component resistance of the empty cell
R component
can be respectively determined from the intercepts of the Nyquist plots on the Re(Z) axis in Fig. 5 (a) and (b) [22]. From Fig. 5 (a), because of the increased path of length along which ions move through the separators, the resistance shows that
R cell
shifts to higher resistance values with the larger thickness of separators. The component resistance
R component
can be directly determined by the intercept of Nyquist curve on the horizontal axis from the Fig. 5 (b), which is 0.222 ± 0.005 Ω.

As discussed in Section 2.4.3, to accurately determine the ionic conductivity of the separator immersed in the electrolyte, it is necessary to eliminate the possible error induced by series resistance. In Fig. 5 (c), the
R cell
is plotted as a function of the varied thickness of separators, which ranges from 25 to 100 μm. According to Eq. (12), the fitted slope of the plot in Fig. 5 (c) is calculated as the
d
R cell
d
L s
=
τ e 2
σ 0
ΦA
=
2.913 ± 0.04
×
10 4
Ω m−1. By inputting the area of spacer ( A = 1.96 ×
10
− 4
m 2
), the conductivity of bulk electrolytes (
σ 0
= 0.830 S m−1) taken from [45] and the experimentally measured porosity of the separator ( Φ = 0.52 ), the electrical tortuosity is determined as 1.57. Electrical tortuosity of the same separator was measured as 1.58 using a self-designed copper block setup in [23], almost identical to the electrical tortuosity (1.57) measured in the present study. However, the coin-cell setup used in our research is more convenient and feasible than the method used in [23]. In addition, by extending the fitted line to intercept with the Y-axis shown in Fig. 5 (c), the series resistance of the configured coin cell
R series
is determined as 0.450 Ω. Therefore, the interfacial resistance between the electrode and electrolyte in configured coin cell
R interfacial
can be derived as 0.228 Ω using Eq. (13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical determination of the geometrical and electrical tortuosity using the 3D image-based model
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the 3D microstructure of the separator was first visualised using a 2D image dataset from nanoscale XCT scanning. The porosity was then analysed and compared with those given by the supplier. Subsequently, the geometrical and electrical tortuosities of the separator were determined numerically, as described briefly in Sections 2.5.2 and 2.5.3, which are detailed below. The module of the Pore Centroid Tortuosity in the Avizo was used to determine the geometrical tortuosity. The module of Formation Factor Experiment Simulation embedded in the XLab package of Avizo was used to calculate the effective electrical conductivity of the separator immersed in the electrolyte by the FVM described in Section 2.5.3, based on which the electrical tortuosity was determined by Eq. (10).

Before the 3D microstructure of the separator was used to determine tortuosity, it was used to analyse the porosity of the separator to validate the image-based model method by comparing the porosity provided by the supplier and the porosity data measured in Section 3.2. With the help of software in Avizo, the 3D microstructure of the separator was reconstructed in Fig. 6 (a). The macroscopic porosity measured by Avizo is 53.3 %, comparable to the porosity of 55 % from the supplier and 52.0 % measured using the density measurement method described in Section 2.4.1. Therefore, it can be reasonably inferred that the present nanoscale XCT can accurately determine the separator's porosity.

Due to the intrinsic anisotropic properties of the Celgard 2500 separator, the tortuosities need to be analysed in three orthogonal directions, namely X, Y and Z. Based on the PCM, the geometrical tortuosities of the separator are respectively 1.06, 2.68 and 1.10. Furthermore, the electrical tortuosities are 1.20, 3.23 and 1.24, respectively, determined using a numerical method to simulate the electrical conductivity experiment via Avizo. The electrical potential distributions inside the pore phase of the separator are shown in Fig. 6 (b), (c) and (d). It can be observed that tortuosity along the Y direction (MD) is much higher than those in the other two directions due to the uniaxial stretch of the separator experienced in MD. In addition, the reconstructed 3D microstructure of the porous separator can be used not only to calculate the geometrical tortuosity but also to support the microstructure design of future separators. Lastly, based on the observation of the results for the geometrical and electrical tortuosity of the separator from numerical simulations, a more general relationship between the geometrical and physical tortuosities is proposed herein by introducing a phenomenological factor ( β ), i.e.
(20)
τ g
= βτ
where τ refers to the tortuosity related to a specific physical transport phenomenon, including fluid permeation, electrical conduction, molecule diffusion and heat conduction [41]. The value of physical tortuosity depends on the specific physical phenomenon under consideration. For example, phenomena such as electrical conduction, molecular diffusion, and heat conduction can yield the same tortuosity values due to their same mathematic forms of constitutive relations described by Ohm's law, Fick's law and Fourier's law, respectively. However, fluid permeation in a porous medium involves the bulk movement of the fluid volume driven by the pressure gradient and the momentum transfer of the fluid flow driven by the velocity gradient.

In the present study, the value of electrical tortuosity can be determined using various methods, e.g. porosity-tortuosity relationship, EIS experiment and 3D microstructure-based simulation. The variability in results arises because each method captures different aspects of the material's microstructure and transport properties. However, those physical tortuosities are intrinsically determined by the pore geometry of the porous medium, which is represented using geometric tortuosity. Therefore, based on the geometrical tortuosity (
τ g
) and electrical tortuosity (
τ e
) determined in Avizo, the electrical phenomenological factors
β e
in our study can be determined as 1.13, 1.21, and 1.13 along X, Y, and Z directions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparisons of porosity, tortuosity and effective transport coefficient values obtained using different methods
ce:section.ce:para: It is evident that the geometrical tortuosity is the smallest, while the electrical tortuosity determined by the experimental method is larger than the electrical tortuosity determined by the numerical method to simulate the electrical conduction. It can be explained that the very fine fibrils of the separator Celgard 2500 cannot be captured in the 3D microstructural model due to the limitation of the resolution of the nanoscale XCT, which also explains the observation that the experimentally determined porosity of the separator (52 %) is less than the porosity measured from the nanoscale XCT method (53.3 %).

In Table 2, the effective transport coefficient ( δ ) along Z-direction derived from the EIS test is 0.211, which is approximately 47 % and 52 % of the effective transport coefficient obtained from the analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations (
δ
e _ Maxwell
= 0.449 ,
δ
e _ Brugg
= 0.409 ), respectively, and 61 % of the effective transport coefficient from the electrical tortuosity using nanoscale XCT (
δ
e _ XCT _ Z
= 0.347 ). In addition, due to the intrinsic thin thickness of the separator itself, it is difficult to experimentally determine the tortuosity of the separator along the in-plane directions (i.e. X and Y directions). However, the in-plane tortuosities of the separator can be determined using the numerical method to simulate the electrical conduction. Therefore, by assuming the value of the electrical tortuosity determined by the experimental method is proportional to that of electrical tortuosity determined by the numerical method along each specific direction (wherein
τ
e _ Exp _ Z
= 1.27
τ
e _ XCT _ Z
), the electrical tortuosities along the X and Y directions using the experimental method can be predicted as 1.52 and 4.09, respectively, when the same ratio value (1.27) along the Z direction is used. When comparing the obtained electrical tortuosities from the Maxwell and Bruggeman equations and XCT modelling method with the experimental value (
τ
e _ XCT _ Z
= 1.57 ) in TTD, the percentage differences are 29 %, 26 % and 21 %, respectively. Therefore, the tortuosity determined by analytical and empirical equations and numerical simulation overestimates the performance of LIB in electrochemical modelling, and the EIS testing method is the most appropriate method to determine the electrical tortuosity of the separator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para:
In this paper, the tortuosities of separators, which play a vital role in the homogeneous modelling of the LIB, were determined based on the analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations, EIS experimental method and numerical simulation from 3D microstructure of nanoscale XCT images. The EIS experimental method is used to determine the electrical tortuosity of the separator and compared with the analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations and numerical modelling method, which gives a relationship of (
τ
e _ Maxwell
<
τ
e _ Brugg
<
τ
e _ XCT
<
τ
e _ Exp
). Furthermore, a phenomenological factor ( β ) is proposed to correlate the relationship between geometrical tortuosity and physical tortuosity in a porous medium, whereby the electrical phenomenological factor is obtained based on the geometrical tortuosity and electrical tortuosity of the 3D microstructure of the separator using numerical simulation in Avizo software.

Although it is straightforward and convenient to determine the tortuosity of the separator using the analytical Maxwell and empirical Bruggeman equations, it is not accurate to represent the tortuosity of separators with complex microstructure, especially when the two separators have similar porosity but different microstructure.

The electrical tortuosity of the separator was determined using configured coin cells by EIS testing. The method proposed here can be standardised to measure the tortuosity of separators in LIB, and meanwhile, it can also be used for thin and porous insulating membranes. In addition, the present study also showed that, to experimentally determine electrical tortuosity, the porosity of the separator also needs to be accurately measured rather than using the porosity provided by the manufacturing supplier. These porosity values have a relationship of (
Φ exp
<
Φ XCT
<
Φ supplier
).

The determination of the electrical tortuosity using microstructure obtained from XCT has the advantage of predicting the tortuosity along the in-plane and through-plane directions, while the experimental method can only measure its tortuosity along the through-plane direction. With the help of 3D microstructure, the geometrical tortuosity of the separator can be determined using PCM, which can support the design of future separators. In addition, the intrinsic anisotropic properties of the studied separator can be observed. Furthermore, by determining the proportional relationship between experimentally-determined and XCT-determined electrical tortuosities along the TTD, the electrical tortuosity of the separator determined by the experimental method along X and Y directions can be predicted. However, compared with electrical tortuosity obtained from the EIS experimental method, the electrical tortuosity determined numerically based on the nanoscale XCT is generally smaller, i.e. (
τ
e _ XCT
<
τ
e _ Exp
), due to the limitation of the length resolution of the nanoscale XCT, which cannot capture the fine fibrils of the separator in the XCT image and the FE model, the separator model is less sinuous than the actual separator.

This study demonstrates the advantages and limitations of different methods in determining the tortuosity of the LIB's separator. By incorporating the experimental method to determine the tortuosity, the nanoscale XCT's limitation can be compensated by manually adding fibres to reconstruct the microstructure of the separator before further conducting mechanical or electrochemical modelling.
